raw_case:
Interstitial lung disease in a patient with anti-eIF2B antibodies-positive systemic sclerosis: A case report and literature review
We report the case of a 76-year-old male patient with systemic sclerosis positive for anti-eukaryotic initiation factor 2B (eIF2B) antibodies. He presented to our hospital with dyspnea on exertion and, following a comprehensive physical examination, was diagnosed with interstitial lung disease associated with systemic sclerosis. Furthermore, systemic sclerosis was positive for the anti-eIF2B antibody. The presence of anti-eIF2B antibodies in systemic sclerosis is very rare, occurring in only 1–2.5 % of cases and seldom reported. Similar to our case, systemic sclerosis with positive anti-eIF2B antibodies has been reported to be more likely to be complicated by interstitial lung disease. Herein, we discuss our case in detail and summarize the previous findings.1

Introduction

Systemic sclerosis (SSc) is an immunological disorder that causes fibrosis and microvascular damage to the skin and organs throughout the body [1]. Various autoantibodies are used to diagnose SSc, and identifying these autoantibodies is important because the complications and course of SSc can vary substantially depending on the type of autoantibody present. Although more than 90 % of autoantibodies in SSc are antinuclear antibodies, some cases test negative for these and instead present with anti-eukaryotic initiation factor 2 B (eIF2B) antibodies [2]. Anti-eIF2B antibodies were first identified as an autoantibody for SSc in 2016 [3], and there have been few reports of SSc associated with these autoantibodies [[3], [4], [5], [6], [7]]. Patients with anti-eIF2B antibodies-positive SSc are prone to developing interstitial lung disease (ILD) [5,8]; but the details are not well understood. We report a rare case of a patient diagnosed with anti-eIF2B antibody-positive SSc and determined to have ILD after a thorough examination.2

Case report

A 76-year-old, male patient presented to our hospital with dyspnea on exertion. Six months ago, he consulted his family doctor about the dyspnea. He underwent a chest X-ray and was suspected of having interstitial lung disease (ILD). The ILD had not been closely investigated, and he was followed-up by his family doctor. He was referred to our hospital six months after his symptoms first appeared.The dyspnea was classified as 2 by Hugh-Jones classification and was not accompanied by other symptoms, such as orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, chest pain or cough. He was aware of edema and Raynaud's phenomenon causing discoloration in his fingers from the same period. He had a history of gastroesophageal reflux disease, for which he was taking a proton pump inhibitor. He had no other medications, either currently or in the past. The patient was a current smoker and had a smoking history of 30 packs/year. His occupation was a school teacher. He had no history of exposure to fumes or dust, and he had never had pets. He had no family history of ILD.The patient's temperature was 36.3 °C, blood pressure was 111/70 mmHg, heart rate was 75/min, respiratory rate was 18/min, and SpO2 was 96 % on room air. Physical examination revealed skin thickening on the face, chest, upper arms, and fingers (Fig. 1A–D). The Rodnan score was 27: 2 for face, 2 for anterior chest, 1 for abdomen, 1 for both upper arms, 2 for both forearms, 2 for both hands, 3 for both fingers, 0 for both thighs, 1 for both legs, and 2 for both foots. There was no arthralgia or myalgia, and no neurological findings. Fine crackles ware predominantly heard in both lower back regions. Mild pitting edema of both lower legs was noted. Chest computed tomography during the inspiration phase demonstrated subpleural reticular pattern (red arrow), traction bronchiectasis (blue arrow), and consolidation (yellow arrow) (Fig. 2A–C). Pulmonary function tests indicated a reduced forced vital capacity of 2.13 L (63.7 % predicted) and diffusing capacity of lung for carbon monoxide (48.7 % predicted) (Table 1). Electrocardiography and echocardiography showed no evidence of pulmonary hypertension or heart dysfunction (Table 2). Systemic sclerosis (SSc) was strongly suspected; however, tests for anti-Scl-70 and anti-centromere antibodies were negative. Minor antibodies were also tested. Blood tests results were positive for anti-eIF2B and anti-SS-A/Ro52 antibodies, and the bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (BALF) showed an increased percentage of eosinophils (Table 3). α-streptococcus was cultured in small quantities, but were determined to be commensal bacteria. Based on these findings, the patient was diagnosed with ILD and diffuse cutaneous SSc, positive for anti-eIF2B antibodies. He was administered nintedanib and followed up at our hospital.3

Discussion

We encountered a patient with systemic sclerosis positive for anti-eIF2B antibodies. The presence of anti-eIF2B antibodies in SSc is very rare. If SSc is strongly suspected but major antibodies are negative, minor antibodies should be tested.Since the first report of SSc with anti-eIF2B antibody in 2016, 28 cases have been reported to date in our search [[3], [4], [5], [6], [7]]. The median age was 48 years, and 67.9 % of patients were female. Ninety-two point nine percent patients had ILD, and 71.4 % of them exhibited diffuse SSc (Table 4). However, as none of these were case reports, we did not have detailed information regarding these cases.The eIF2B is a translation initiation factor consisting of five subunits (α to ε). It is present in the cytoplasm and regulates the initiation of protein synthesis [9]. Decreased eIF2B activity has been implicated in neurodegenerative diseases, metabolic syndromes, and cancer [10]. The frequency of anti-eIF2B antibodies in SSc is reported to be 1–2.5 % [[3], [4], [5], [6], [7]]. Molecular mimicry is one of the many hypothesized causes of SSc. Molecular mimicry refers to the structural similarity between a pathogen antigen and a host antigen that were originally unrelated, leading to an immune cross-reaction and causing autoimmune disease [10]. Additionally, SSc caused by anti-eIF2B antibodies can be explained by this molecular mimicry [11]. In 58 % of patients with SSc, IgG antibodies show abnormal reactivity to specific Epstein-Barr virus antigens, which may produce a protein similar to eIF2B, suggesting that Epstein-Barr virus molecular mimicry may target autoantigens and cause SSc [12].We consulted a collagen specialist immediately after the patient's visit. In addition to SSc, mixed connective tissue disease and Sjögren's syndrome were initially considered as differential diagnoses in this case. After a thorough investigation of even minor antibodies, he was finally diagnosed with SSc. This case was characterized by a clear increase in eosinophils to 15 % in the BALF. No reports were found of evaluated eosinophil levels in the BALF of patients with SSc. The patient had no history of allergic disease, including asthma. It was more reasonable to consider ILD associated with SSc than eosinophilic pneumonia. However, the possibility that elevated eosinophil levels in BALF were due to the characteristic of SSc with a positive anti-eIF2b antibodies or drug-induced pneumonia caused by proton pomp inhibitor cannot be ruled out. Therefore, it is necessary to collect additional cases for further.ILD was diagnosed with probable UIP pattern in the chest computed tomography. Pulmonary function tests indicated a reduced forced vital capacity. There is no absolute established treatment for ILD associated with SSc, and each patient's treatment should be individualized [13]. The collagen specialist determined that immunosuppressive drugs, such as mycophenolate and steroids, were not indicated in this case at this stage. Therefore, we administered nintedanib to the patient with ILD associated with SSc. A whole-body CT scan was performed as a screening, but no findings suspicious for cancer were observed. This is the first detailed case report of ILD caused by anti-eIF2B positive SSc, including imaging data, pulmonary function tests and BALF. The clinical presentation and course of this case did not differ significantly from those of common SSc. Reports of SSc positivity for anti-eIF2B antibodies should be collected.4

Conclusion

If SSc is strongly suspected, even when anti-Scl-70 antibodies or anti-centromere antibodies are negative, minor antibodies should be investigated.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

Naoya Aoshiba: Writing – original draft. Kazutoshi Toriyama: Writing – original draft. Shohei Yamashita: Writing – review & editing. Nao Shioiri: Writing – review & editing. Yuko Iwata: Writing – review & editing. Tomonori Uruma: Writing – review & editing. Shinji Abe: Writing – review & editing. Kenji Tsushima: Writing – review & editing.

Declaration of competing interest

The authors declare that have no known competing financial interests or personal relationship that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 76-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Dyspnea on exertion
- **History of Present Illness:** Dyspnea for 6 months, classified as Hugh-Jones grade 2; associated with edema and Raynaud's phenomenon; no orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, chest pain, or cough.
- **Past Medical History:** Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), treated with a proton pump inhibitor.
- **Personal History:** Current smoker, 30 pack-years; former school teacher; no history of significant environmental exposures; no pets.
- **Family History:** None relevant to interstitial lung disease (ILD).
- **Physical Examination:** Skin thickening (face, chest, arms, fingers); fine crackles in lower back regions; mild lower extremity pitting edema; Rodnan score = 27. No arthralgia, myalgia, or neurological findings. 
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - **Imaging:** Chest CT with subpleural reticular changes, traction bronchiectasis, and consolidation indicating probable UIP pattern.
  - **Pulmonary Function Tests:** Reduced forced vital capacity (63.7% predicted), reduced diffusing capacity of the lung for carbon monoxide (48.7% predicted).
  - **Laboratory Findings:** Positive for anti-eIF2B and anti-SS-A/Ro52 antibodies; negative for anti-Scl-70 and anti-centromere antibodies. BALF showed increased eosinophils (15%); α-streptococcus cultured (commensal).
  - **Cardiac Workup:** No evidence of pulmonary hypertension or heart dysfunction via electrocardiography and echocardiography.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Interstitial lung disease (ILD):** Subpleural reticular pattern and traction bronchiectasis on CT imaging, reduced pulmonary function metrics, dyspnea, and fine crackles all supported ILD as the primary diagnosis. Probable UIP pattern was diagnosed on imaging. 
   - **Excluded eosinophilic pneumonia:** Despite elevated eosinophils in BALF, the absence of allergic history or additional features (e.g., asthma, fevers, acute onset chest symptoms) made an eosinophilic condition less likely.
   - **Excluded secondary causes of ILD:** Smoking history considered but ruled out other etiologies due to findings specifically tied to systemic sclerosis.
   
2. **Systemic sclerosis (SSc):** Strongly suspected due to skin thickening, Raynaud’s phenomenon, and GERD. Findings on CT imaging and pulmonary function tests suggested ILD, which is a common complication of SSc.
   - **Confirmed SSc:** Detected autoantibodies (anti-eIF2B, anti-SS-A/Ro52), clinical findings, and BALF eosinophilia supported a diagnosis of SSc, despite negative anti-Scl-70 and anti-centromere antibodies. Anti-eIF2B antibody positivity, although rare, was diagnostic.

3. **Differentiated from mixed connective tissue disease (MCTD) and Sjögren’s syndrome:** Tested based on clinical and antibody profiles. Exclusion was based on absence of diagnostic features for these syndromes and clinical correlation with SSc-related ILD.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Interstitial lung disease (ILD) associated with systemic sclerosis (SSc), positive for anti-eIF2B antibodies. Diagnosis was confirmed by clinical findings, a specific antibody profile, imaging, and pulmonary function tests. Eosinophilia in BALF was a notable but unexplained finding related to either SSc or other minor comorbid factors (e.g., drug-induced).
generate_case.diagnosis_results: ILD associated with anti-eIF2B antibodies-positive systemic sclerosis

results.content:
### Resoning:

Let's start by understanding the patient's chief complaint and history. The patient is a 76-year-old male with dyspnea on exertion for six months, classified as Hugh-Jones grade 2. He also has edema and Raynaud's phenomenon, which are important symptoms to consider in the differential diagnosis.

First, I need to consider the possible causes of dyspnea in this patient. Given his age and smoking history, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) comes to mind, but the physical examination and other findings suggest more is going on here.

The physical examination reveals skin thickening on the face, chest, arms, and fingers, which is suggestive of scleroderma or another connective tissue disease. The presence of Raynaud's phenomenon further supports this possibility, as it is commonly associated with scleroderma.

Looking at the imaging studies, the chest CT shows subpleural reticular changes, traction bronchiectasis, and consolidation, which are indicative of a usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP) pattern. This is important because UIP is often associated with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF), but it can also be seen in connective tissue diseases like scleroderma.

The pulmonary function tests show reduced forced vital capacity (FVC) and diffusing capacity of the lung for carbon monoxide (DLCO), which is consistent with restrictive lung disease and impaired gas exchange. This aligns with the imaging findings suggestive of interstitial lung disease (ILD).

The laboratory findings are interesting. The patient has positive anti-eIF2B and anti-SS-A/Ro52 antibodies. Anti-eIF2B antibodies are associated with anti-synthetase syndrome, which is a subgroup of inflammatory myopathies that can present with ILD, arthritis, myositis, and other features. Anti-SS-A/Ro52 antibodies are commonly found in Sjögren's syndrome and other connective tissue diseases.

The absence of anti-Scl-70 and anti-centromere antibodies makes systemic sclerosis (the autoimmune disease commonly referred to as scleroderma) less likely, although the clinical features suggest scleroderma-like changes.

The bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (BALF) shows increased eosinophils (15%), which could suggest an eosinophilic pneumonia or other eosinophilic lung disease. However, the presence of α-streptococcus in culture might be a commensal and not necessarily pathogenic in this context.

The cardiac workup is normal, with no evidence of pulmonary hypertension or heart dysfunction, which helps to rule out cardiac causes of dyspnea and edema.

Now, let's try to integrate all these findings. The patient has features suggestive of a connective tissue disease, with skin thickening, Raynaud's phenomenon, and positive autoantibodies. The presence of anti-eIF2B antibodies points towards anti-synthetase syndrome, which can present with ILD, arthritis, and myositis.

However, the UIP pattern on chest CT is more typical of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, which is usually not associated with anti-synthetase syndrome. Anti-synthetase syndrome typically presents with nonspecific interstitial pneumonia (NSIP) or organizing pneumonia patterns, not UIP.

But, there are overlapping features, and occasionally, UIP can be seen in connective tissue diseases. The positive anti-SS-A/Ro52 antibodies also complicate the picture, as these are associated with various conditions, including Sjögren's syndrome and other autoimmune diseases.

The edema could be due to heart failure, but since the cardiac evaluation is normal, it might be related to the underlying systemic disease, possibly hypoalbuminemia if there is significant inflammation or liver involvement, but that's speculative without more data.

The dyspnea on exertion and the pulmonary function test results are concerning for significant lung disease affecting the patient's quality of life.

Given all this, I need to consider the differential diagnosis:

1. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) with UIP pattern on CT, but the presence of autoantibodies makes this less likely, as IPF is typically idiopathic with no associated autoimmune disease.

2. Connective tissue disease-associated interstitial lung disease (CTD-ILD), specifically in the context of anti-synthetase syndrome due to the positive anti-eIF2B antibodies.

3. Scleroderma or another type of systemic sclerosis, given the skin thickening and Raynaud's phenomenon, but the antibody profile isn't typical.

4. Eosinophilic pneumonia, given the elevated eosinophils in BALF, but this usually presents with different clinical features and isn't aligning well with the overall picture.

Considering the UIP pattern and the presence of autoantibodies, it's possible that this patient has CTD-ILD with features overlapping between IPF and anti-synthetase syndrome.

However, the gold standard for diagnosing UIP pattern is a surgical lung biopsy, but given the risks associated with that procedure, especially in an older patient, it may not be feasible.

Alternatively, a combination of clinical, radiological, and functional findings can be used to make a diagnosis, as per the established guidelines for IPF.

But in this case, the presence of autoantibodies complicates the picture, suggesting an autoimmune etiology.

Perhaps the patient has a form of CTD-ILD that resembles IPF.

Alternatively, it could be anti-synthetase syndrome with a UIP pattern, which is less common but possible.

I would consider consulting with a rheumatologist to evaluate the autoantibody profile and consider the possibility of an overlapping connective tissue disease.

The skin thickening could be due to scleroderma-like changes in the context of another autoimmune disease.

The edema might be related to hypoalbuminemia if there is significant inflammation or liver involvement, but again, that's speculative.

Given the complexity of the case, a multidisciplinary discussion involving pulmonology, rheumatology, and possibly dermatology would be beneficial to整合 all the clinical, radiological, and laboratory findings and reach a consensus on the diagnosis and management plan.

In summary, the most likely diagnosis is connective tissue disease-associated interstitial lung disease, possibly related to anti-synthetase syndrome, with a UIP pattern on imaging.

### Answer:

Connective tissue disease-associated interstitial lung disease, likely anti-synthetase syndrome with UIP pattern。

<Step 2> Eosinophilic pneumonia was excluded due to the absence of allergic history, asthma, fevers, or acute onset chest symptoms, despite elevated eosinophils in BALF.
<Step 3> Secondary causes of ILD were excluded, including smoking-related etiologies, as findings were specifically tied to systemic sclerosis (SSc).

<Step 4> Systemic sclerosis (SSc) was strongly suspected due to skin thickening, Raynaud’s phenomenon, GERD, and ILD findings on CT imaging and pulmonary function tests, which are common complications of SSc.

<Step 5> SSc was confirmed based on detected autoantibodies (anti-eIF2B, anti-SS-A/Ro52), clinical findings, and BALF eosinophilia, despite negative anti-Scl-70 and anti-centromere antibodies, with anti-eIF2B antibody positivity being diagnostic.
<Step 6> Mixed connective tissue disease (MCTD) and Sjögren’s syndrome were excluded based on the absence of diagnostic features for these syndromes and clinical correlation with SSc-related ILD.

<Step 7> The final diagnosis was interstitial lung disease (ILD) associated with systemic sclerosis (SSc), positive for anti-eIF2B antibodies, confirmed by clinical findings, specific antibody profile, imaging, and pulmonary function tests.
<Step 8> Eosinophilia in BALF was noted as a notable but unexplained finding potentially related to SSc or other minor comorbid factors, such as drug-induced effects.
